title.en: Debugging With AI Without Going in Circles

description.en: Most people debug with AI badly: they paste 'it doesn't work' and get a confident wrong guess, then spiral for an hour. Learn the disciplined loop — exact error, minimal repro, expected vs actual — and how to break the confident-fix spiral before it eats your afternoon.

story_variants.en:
## The Hour You Lost to 'It Doesn't Work'

You hit a bug. You paste your function into the AI with the message: *"this doesn't work, fix it."* The AI — which has never seen your error, your data, or your stack trace — does the only thing it can do: it *guesses*, confidently. It rewrites three lines, changes a variable name, and says "This should fix it!" You run it. Still broken. You reply "still broken." It guesses again, just as confidently, in a slightly different direction. Forty minutes later you have a function that's been rewritten four times, you understand it *less* than when you started, and the original bug is still there.

This is the **confident-fix spiral**, and almost everyone falls into it. The AI is not malfunctioning — you starved it of the one thing that makes debugging possible: *evidence*. A model that can't see the error can only pattern-match on vibes. Garbage in, confident garbage out.

Good debugging with AI is a discipline, not a vibe. Here is the loop.

## The Disciplined Debugging Loop

**1. Give the EXACT error — the full traceback, not a paraphrase.** "It crashes" tells the AI nothing. `IndexError: list index out of range` on line 14 tells it almost everything. The traceback names the *file*, the *line*, the *exception type*, and the *call chain that led there*. Paste all of it. A paraphrase like "some index thing" throws away the exact signal that would have localized the bug in one shot.

**2. Give a MINIMAL reproducible example — not the whole repo.** Strip the bug down to the smallest snippet that still fails. This does two things: it often reveals the bug *to you* before you even ask, and it stops the AI from drowning in 400 lines of irrelevant context and guessing about the wrong part.

**3. State EXPECTED vs ACTUAL.** "I expected `[1, 2, 3]`, I got `[1, 2]`." This single sentence converts a vague complaint into a precise target. The AI now debugs *toward a known answer* instead of toward your mood.

**4. When a fix fails, say PRECISELY how.** Not "still broken." Instead: "Now it raises `KeyError: 'age'` on line 9 instead." Each failure is data. "Still broken" deletes the data and forces another blind guess. Precise failure reports turn the AI from a slot machine into an instrument.

**5. Break the spiral after two failed attempts.** This is the rule that saves your afternoon. If two real fixes haven't worked, **stop asking for fixes.** The AI is now guessing in the dark and you're following it there. Take back the wheel: form a hypothesis yourself, add a `print()` to check one assumption, or bisect — comment out half the code and see if the bug survives. The AI is a brilliant rubber duck, but *you* steer.

## Bad Prompt vs Good Prompt

| Bad debugging prompt | Good debugging prompt |
|----------------------|-----------------------|
| "It doesn't work, fix it." | "`IndexError: list index out of range` on line 14. Here's the 6-line repro." |
| "Something's wrong with my loop." | "I expected sum=10, got sum=6. Loop runs 3 times instead of 4." |
| "Still broken." | "Your fix removed the IndexError but now returns `[]` instead of `[1,2,3]`." |
| Pastes the entire 400-line file | Pastes the 8 lines that actually fail, with sample input |
| "Just make it work somehow." | "Here's the traceback + expected vs actual. What's the most likely cause?" |

Notice the pattern: every good prompt hands the AI *evidence*. Every bad prompt hands it a *feeling*.

## The AI Is a Rubber Duck You Can Talk Back

The classic debugging trick is to explain your bug to a rubber duck on your desk — half the time you solve it mid-sentence, because articulating the problem forces you to confront your assumptions. The AI is that duck, except it answers. The value isn't that it magically knows your bug; it's that *preparing a good question* (exact error, minimal repro, expected vs actual) is itself most of the debugging. If you find you can't write the minimal repro, that's not a failure — that's the bug telling you that you don't yet understand where it lives. Go find out, then ask.

> **Key idea:** The AI can't debug what it can't see. Feed it the exact error, a minimal repro, and expected-vs-actual — and when two fixes fail, stop the spiral and form your own hypothesis. You are the debugger; the AI is your fastest assistant, not your replacement.

nodes.label: The Disciplined Debugging Loop
children: 4. Say precisely how a fix failed

left.label: Feeling: 'It doesn't work, fix it.'  ->  confident wrong guess

right.label: Evidence: 'IndexError line 14 + 6-line repro + expected vs actual'  ->  targeted fix

description.en: Every good debugging prompt hands the AI EVIDENCE. Every bad one hands it a FEELING.

executionSteps.description: len is 2, so it indexes -1, 0, 1 — that -1 wraps, then index 2 is out of range.

executionSteps.output: IndexError: list index out of range
executionSteps.description: The EXACT error + line is the signal. Paste this, not 'it doesn't work'.

output: Two confident fixes = the AI is guessing in the dark.
Take the wheel: hypothesize -> add a print() -> bisect.
The AI is your rubber duck. YOU are the debugger.

instruction.en: Be the debugger. This function should return the last 3 items of a list. Running it on [10, 20, 30, 40] raises 'IndexError: list index out of range' (see the comment). Read the EXACT error, localize it to the indexing line, and fix the off-by-one so it returns the last 3 items. With the given data the answer is [20, 30, 40]. Expected output: [20, 30, 40]

practice_challenges.starter_code:
def last_three(items):
    result = []
    # ERROR when run: IndexError: list index out of range
    # The loop indexes one position too far (off-by-one).
    for i in range(len(items) - 3, len(items) + 1):
        result.append(items[i])
    return result

print(last_three([10, 20, 30, 40]))

en: Read the EXACT error: 'IndexError: list index out of range' means you indexed past the end of the list.
Localize it: valid indices for a length-4 list are 0..3. The loop runs while i is in range(1, 5), so i=4 is out of range — that's the off-by-one.
range(start, stop) already stops BEFORE stop. Change range(len(items) - 3, len(items) + 1) to range(len(items) - 3, len(items)).
After the fix, i takes values 1, 2, 3 -> items[1], items[2], items[3] -> [20, 30, 40].

q.en: You paste your function with 'this doesn't work, fix it.' Why does the AI give a confident WRONG guess?

en: The model is broken and needs restarting
You gave it a feeling instead of evidence — with no error or repro it can only pattern-match on vibes
Python errors are random
The AI is deliberately misleading you

q.en: What should you paste instead of paraphrasing the error as 'some index thing'?

en: The full, exact traceback — file, line, exception type, and call chain
A screenshot of your whole screen
Just the function name
The number of times it crashed

q.en: After a fix fails, why is 'still broken' worse than 'now it raises KeyError: age on line 9'?

en: 'Still broken' is shorter and that's bad
The precise failure is DATA that steers the next fix; 'still broken' deletes it and forces another blind guess
There is no difference
KeyError is always more important than other errors

q.en: Two real fixes from the AI have both failed. What's the disciplined next move?

en: Ask for a third fix, then a fourth, until one sticks
Stop the spiral — form your own hypothesis, add a print(), or bisect the code yourself
Rewrite the whole file from scratch
Paste the entire repo and ask again
